questions.text: I find that even after receiving surprising news, my body remains relaxed and my breathing stays even.
I am happy to let someone else be the spokesperson.
I return borrowed items promptly because outstanding obligations weigh on me.
I can listen to someone describe their suffering and maintain complete emotional equilibrium.
I believe I process stimuli at a deeper level than most, which is why everything feels so vivid.
I hold the view that the world needs more people who give without calculating what they get back.
I find that approaching human tragedy as a problem to be analyzed and solved is the most productive use of my capabilities.
I can usually tell when someone has been crying even if they have composed themselves.
I tend to weigh how a decision affects me before considering how it affects others.
I routinely offer to drive others to appointments, even when it disrupts my own plans.
I feel that the moral demand of suffering is the experience that keeps my ethical life from becoming a matter of routine and forces it to remain a matter of active engagement.
I hold the view that the suffering of anyone, anywhere, deserves to be weighed as real and significant in my moral reasoning.
I do not naturally read the social meaning behind gift-giving or gesture exchanges.
I tend to keep my best ideas for my own projects rather than giving them away.
I believe that strong leadership requires the willingness to override dissent when needed.
After an emotionally charged event, I feel like I need to recover as if from physical exertion.
I routinely negotiate for the best possible outcome for myself.
I often help resolve group disagreements by finding common ground.
My inner emotional life is relatively smooth and consistent.
I am usually the first to stand up and take charge in a new situation.
I run errands as they occur to me, sometimes doubling back, and that's fine by me.
I set aside time regularly to review what's working and what needs adjustment.
I pay bills and handle obligations when I notice them rather than setting up automation.
I find that once an emotion takes hold, it commands my full attention.
I hold the view that the seriousness with which I respond to suffering is the most reliable way I have to check whether I am living according to my stated values.
I find it hard to enjoy something if I know someone else had to go without.
I am comfortable making unpopular decisions if I believe they are correct.
I see myself as a valued crew member of whatever ship I am on.
I can sense when a group chat message was drafted and redrafted before being sent.
I use consistent formats and templates for recurring communications.
I hold the view that my even temperament is a reflection of my biology, not a choice I make.
I pick up on when someone is being intentionally ambiguous to keep their options open.
I find that keeping my emotional reactions in check during conversations about pain allows me to evaluate the situation more accurately.
I find that I am more attuned to ideas being discussed than to the feelings in the room.
I tend to work on whatever grabs my attention rather than following a priority list.
I rarely pick up on when a person's compliment is backhanded.
